claims.claim: CSS masonry layout uses `display: grid-lanes` (not a `grid-template-rows: masonry` value), as it is defined in Level 3 of the CSS Grid Layout specification under the grid-lanes model.
claims.quote: Level 3 of the CSS grid layout specification defines masonry layout (also known as grid-lanes layout)

claims.claim: Masonry layout is an experimental technology not yet at Baseline, meaning it does not work in some of the most widely-used browsers and should not be used in production without a fallback.
claims.quote: This feature is not Baseline because it does not work in some of the most widely-used browsers.

claims.claim: In browsers that do not support masonry, regular grid auto-placement is used as the automatic fallback behavior, requiring no explicit fallback code.
claims.quote: In browsers that do not support masonry, regular grid auto-placement will be used instead.

claims.claim: On the masonry (stacking) axis, items in later rows rise up to fill gaps left by shorter items, rather than staying aligned to a strict grid row.
claims.quote: On the stacking axis, rather than sticking to a strict grid with gaps being left after shorter items, the items in the following row rise up to fill the gaps.

claims.claim: Items with definite placement are positioned before the masonry algorithm runs, and items can span multiple column tracks using the `span` keyword.
claims.quote: Items can span multiple tracks using the span keyword. Items with definite placement are positioned before masonry layout occurs.
